PRCP-1016 - HEART DISEASE PREDICTION

1. Project Overview & Environment Setup

1.1 Project Objective

The primary objective of this project is to predict the likelihood of Cardiovascular Disease (CVD) and to develop an accurate, high-sensitivity Machine Learning model based on patient clinical indicators.

By identifying high-risk individuals early, healthcare institutions can transition from reactive treatment to proactive prevention, ultimately minimizing life-threatening complications like cardiac arrests and strokes.

This project includes:

  1. Data Analysis
  2. Predictive Modeling
  3. Model Comparison
  4. Suggestions for prevention of life threatening heart disease.

2.2 Observations

The dataset contains a mix of numerical (e.g., blood pressure) and categorical/discrete variables (represented numerically, like chest pain type and fasting blood sugar).

The dataset contains no null values and represents a patient population where nearly 1 in 6 individuals show elevated fasting blood sugar, and there is a broad range of cardiovascular heath markers, particularly in blood pressure and ST segment slopes.

3. TASK 1: EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS (EDA)

Examine the structure, statistics, and underlying patterns of the dataset.

3.1 Heart Disease Distribution

In [15]:
data1['heart_disease_present'].value_counts()
Out[15]:
heart_disease_present
0    100
1     80
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [16]:
sns.countplot(x='heart_disease_present', data=data1)
plt.title('Heart Disease Distribution')
plt.show()

Insights

The dataset shows a slight inbalance between two classes, where Class 0 (No Heart Disease) is the majority class, with a count of ~100 instances. And Class 1 (Heart Disease Present) is the minority class, with a count of ~80 instances.

The dataset is relatively balanced , though it leans slightly toward "No Heart Disease". While Class 0 is more frequent, the ratio (roughly 5:4) is not skewed enough to require aggressive resampling techniques.

3.2 Numerical Feature Analysis

3.2.1 Age Distribution

In [17]:
sns.histplot(data1['age'], kde=True)
plt.title('Age Distribution')
plt.show()

Insights

The distribution is negatively skewed (left-skewed), meaning the "tail" extends toward the younger ages while the majority of the data points are concentrated on the higher end of the scale, i.e. individuals in the 55-65 age range.

As the chart peaks in the late 50s and 60s, this aligns with the period where heart disease risk increases significantly. The bars representing the 30-45 age group are lower, meaning heart disease in this group is less common.

3.2.2 Gender Distribution

In [18]:
sns.countplot(x='sex', data=data1)
plt.title('Gender Distribution')
plt.show()

Insights

The "Gender Distribution" chart shows a clear disparity. The count for sex 1 (typically representing males in medical datasets) is ~115-120, while sex 0 (typically females) is around ~55-60.

The dataset is imbalanced and the skewed distribution means the findings might not generalize well to the minority. The dataset contains nearly double the number of male subjects compared to the female subjects. A predictive model might achieve higher accuracy for the majority group (sex 1) while performing poorly for the minority group (sex 0).

3.2.3 Gender vs Heart Disease

In [19]:
sns.countplot(x='sex', hue='heart_disease_present', data=data1)
plt.title('Gender vs Heart Disease')
plt.show()

Insights

The dataset is imbalanced, it contains a larger overall sample of males than females.

The dataset shows Males (category 1) has a significantly higher incidence of heart disease (orange bars) compared to females (category 0). For females, the vast majority do not have heart disease, with the "no-disease" group (blue) being roughly four times larger than the disease" group. For males, the distribution is nearly 1:1, meaning a male in this dataset is much more likely to have a heart disease diagnosis than a female.

3.2.4 Chest Pain Type vs Heart Disease

In [20]:
sns.countplot(x='sex', hue='chest_pain_type', data=data1)
plt.title('Chest Pain Type vs Heart Disease')
plt.show()

Insights

The dataset is imbalanced. There is a clear imbalace between Group 0 and Group 1. Group 1 has much larger total count compared to Group 0. Within both groups, the distribution of chest pain types is not uniform. Types 3 and 4 dominate the count, while types 1 and 2 are underrepresented.

Chest pain Type 2 is the most common, followed closely by Type 3. Chest pain Type 4 is the most prevalent (exceeding 50 counts), followed by Type 3. Type 1 chest pain is the rarest category in both groups, providing very little data for the model to learn its specific characteristics.

3.2.5 Cholesterol Distribution

In [21]:
sns.histplot(data['serum_cholesterol_mg_per_dl'], kde=True)
plt.title('Cholesterol Distribution')
plt.show()

Insights

The dataset Clearly shows inbalance in the feature values. The distribution is not perfectly symmetrical. There are very few data points for "normal" cholesterol (under 150 mg/dl) compared to the "high" categories. There are visible outliers on the far right (near 550 mg/dl). In this dataset, these individuals represents high-risk cases.

High serum cholesterol is a primary risk factor for heart disease. Because the bulk of the data sits above the "desirable" level (typically <200 mg/dl), this populaton appears to be at a significant cardiovascular risk. The majority of the sample falls between 200 and 300 mg/dl, which is the range often associated with "borderline high" to "high" cholesterol in clinical settings.

3.2.6 Correlation Heatmap

In [22]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,8))
sns.heatmap(data1.corr(numeric_only=True), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.show()

Insights

This correlation heatmap illustrates the relationships between various clinical features and the presence of heart disease.

The dataset shows a notable correlation between oldpeak_eq_st_depression and slope_of_peak_exercise_st_segment (0.62). This is expected as both are indicators of cardiac stress during physical activity.

Age and max_hear_rate_achieved have a negative correlation (-0.39), reflecting the natural physiological decline of maximum heart rate as one ages.

The variable heart_disease_present shows the strongest positive correlations with : exercise_induced_angina (0.45), num_major_vessels (0.42), chest_pain_type (0.41), and slope_of_peak_exercise_st_segment (0.34).

max_heart_rate_achieved has a strong negative correlation (-0.38) with heart_disease_present , suggesting that lower peak heart rates are more frequently associated with the presence of the disease in this dataset.

le = LabelEncoder()
data1['thal']= le.fit_transform(data1['thal'])
In [26]:
data1.head()
Out[26]:
patient_id slope_of_peak_exercise_st_segment thal resting_blood_pressure chest_pain_type num_major_vessels fasting_blood_sugar_gt_120_mg_per_dl resting_ekg_results serum_cholesterol_mg_per_dl oldpeak_eq_st_depression sex age max_heart_rate_achieved exercise_induced_angina heart_disease_present
0 0z64un 1 1 128 2 0 0 2 308 0.0 1 45 170 0 0
1 ryoo3j 2 1 110 3 0 0 0 214 1.6 0 54 158 0 0
2 yt1s1x 1 1 125 4 3 0 2 304 0.0 1 77 162 1 1
3 l2xjde 1 2 152 4 0 0 0 223 0.0 1 40 181 0 1
4 oyt4ek 3 2 178 1 0 0 2 270 4.2 1 59 145 0 0

Insights

Categorical variable 'thal' is converted into numerical variable using Label Encoding.

LabelEncoder was selected because it is categorical column and machine learning algorithms require numerical inputs. The categorical values were converted into numeric labels while preserving distinct categories. Also, Tree-based ensemble models (like Random Forests) process label-encoded values efficiently without forcing an artificial ordinal relationship to degrade tree splits.

In [27]:
# Checking for outliers
plt.figure(figsize=(11,7))
sns.boxplot(data=data1[['age', 'resting_blood_pressure', 'serum_cholesterol_mg_per_dl', 'max_heart_rate_achieved', 'oldpeak_eq_st_depression']])
plt.show()

Insights

The IQR method is used to identify and cap extreme values. This reduces the impact of unusal observations while preserving the overall data distributuion.

There is a significant difference in the scales of there variables (e.g., Cholesterol goes upto 400+, while ST Depression is near 0).

The Serum Cholesterol variable shows the highest values and the most significant spread. The presence of an outlier at the top end indicates some individuals have exceptionally high cholesterol levels compared to the rest of the group. The Resting Blood Pressure data is more tightly clustered than cholesterol. It also contains atleast one outlier on the higher end, suggesting a few instances of notably high blood pressure readings.

The Max Heart Rate variable appears relatrively normally distributed within its range, with a median that sits roughly in the center of its interquartile range. The Age range is fairly compact, primarily failing between 40 and 60 years old. The ST Depression value is very low and has extremely low variance, with most data points clustered near zero.

In [28]:
# Function to Cap Outliers
def cap_outliers(column):
    Q1 = data1[column].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = data1[column].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    data1[column] = np.where(data1[column] > upper, upper, data1[column])
    data1[column] = np.where(data1[column] < lower, lower, data1[column])

cap_outliers('serum_cholesterol_mg_per_dl')
In [29]:
plt.figure(figsize=(11,7))
sns.boxplot(data=data1[['age', 'resting_blood_pressure', 'serum_cholesterol_mg_per_dl', 'max_heart_rate_achieved', 'oldpeak_eq_st_depression']])
plt.show()

Insights

The dataset shows visible outliears in the resting_blood_pressure (high end) and peak_eq_st_depression (high end) categories. Serum cholesterol has the highest variance and the largest range, extending from ~120 to over 350. Most variables appear relatively symmetrical, though heart_rate_achieved suggests a slight left-skew.

The Serum Cholesterol variable has the highest values and the greatest variance, with whiskers extending from ~130 to over 400. The Resting Blood Pressure shows a tighter distrubution than cholesterol, with a few outliears on the higher end (above 170).

Heart Rate Achieved has a fairly symmetrical distribution between 100 and 200. The Age median appears to be in the mid-50s, with most subjects falling between 45 and 65 years old. ST Depression variable has very low values (mostly near 0) with a significant outlier.

In [30]:
# Splitting dataset
x = data1.drop(['patient_id', 'heart_disease_present'], axis=1)
y = data1['heart_disease_present']

Insights

The 'patient_id' column is removed from the feature set because it is a unique identifier. And it will not continue contributing to predict heart disease, as it could include introducing unnecessary noise into the model.

In [31]:
# Train-Test Split
# Split the dataset into clean development and evaluating environments
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

Insights

The dataset is split into training and testing sets using a 20% test size, leaving 80% for training.

Features (x): All columns except heart_disease_present are retained to act as predictors.

Target (y): The heart_disease_present column is isolated as the label the model will attempt to predict.

In [32]:
# Feature Scaling
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
scaler = StandardScaler()
x_train_scaled=scaler.fit_transform(x_train)
x_test_scaled=scaler.transform(x_test)

Insights

Feature scaling is applied to standardize numerical variables and improve model performance. This prevents critical data leakage and maintains testing data integrity.

StandardScaler was chosen because Distance-based models (such as KNN and Logistic Regression) are sensistive to feature magnitudes and are heavily biased toward features with larger absolute ranges. Standardizing ensures all features contribute equally during model training.

In [33]:
y.value_counts()
Out[33]:
heart_disease_present
0    100
1     80
Name: count, dtype: int64

Insights

The target class is a highly balanced dataset, with a 55.6% to 44.4% distribution split. This do not need resampling model. Synthetic techniques like SMOTE is not required. Standard loss functions will perform well without modifications. Adjusting slgorithm class weights is not needed. The model will not inherently favor one class over another.

TASK 1: DATA ANALYSIS REPORT (EDA)

Key Findings from EDA

Demographic Risk Factors: Males exhibit a disproportionately higher rate of cardiovascular disease instances compared to females within this cohort. Risk trends upward significantly for individuals above 55 years of age.

Physiological Indicators: Patients diagnosed with heart disease show a high correlation with exercise-induced angina, elevated serum cholesterol levels, and higher resting blood pressure.

ECG & Stress Testing: A ST depression (oldpeak_eq_st_depression) value greater than 2.0 combined with a "reversible defect" or "fixed defect" outcome in the thallium stress test (thal) are the strongest positive indicators of underlying coronary artery constraints.

Heart Rate Cap: Patients who reached a lower max_heart_rate_achieved during stress tests despite exertion were highly correlated with positive heart disease diagnoses, pointing to impaired cardiac output.

Data Dimesions

Total columns: 14 distinct features

Target column: Heart Disease presence indicator

Unique identifier: patient_id column

Outlier Detection

Target features: Blood pressure and cholesterol columns

Visual method: Boxplot boundary inspection

Mathematical method: Interquartile Range (IQR) filtering

Univariate Exploration

Demographic Breakdown

Age distribution: Concentrated primarily in mid-fifties range

Gender representation: Skewed heavily towards male records

Class balance: Even distribution of target variables

Bivariate Analysis

Low Max Heart Rate - Linked to Higher Disease Risk

High Oldpeak ST-Depression - Linked to Severe Tissue Stress

Reversible Thallium Defect - Linked to Structural Anomalies

Statistical Distribution Insights

Age & Heart Rate: Age follows a normal distribution

Max heart rate decreases linearly as age increases

Blood Pressure & Cholesterol: Both features show standard right-skewness. They require outlier capping.

Target Balance: The target classes are relatively well-balanced. This eliminates severe class-imbalance bias.

5. TASK 2: MACHINE LEARNING MODEL BUILDING AND EVALUATION

We evaluate three distinct algorithms for clinical diagnostic for a heart disease.

In [34]:
# Import Machine Learning libraries
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
In [35]:
# Import evaluating metrics
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

5.1 Model 1: Logistic Regression

In [36]:
lr = LogisticRegression()
lr.fit(x_train_scaled, y_train)
y_pred_lr = lr.predict(x_test_scaled)
lr_acc = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_lr)
print("Logistic Regression Accuracy:", lr_acc)

Logistic Regression Accuracy: 0.8333333333333334
In [37]:
# Classification report
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_lr))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.75      0.94      0.83        16
           1       0.94      0.75      0.83        20

    accuracy                           0.83        36
   macro avg       0.84      0.84      0.83        36
weighted avg       0.85      0.83      0.83        36
In [38]:
# Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_lr)
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues')
plt.title("Logistic Regression")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.show()

Insights

The Logistic Regression confusion matrix shows an accuracy of 83.33%. It shows strong precision, meaning a positive heart disease diagnosis is highly reliable.

Both classes share an F1-score of 0.83, indicating a generally stable and balanced model across both categories.

The model has high Recall (0.94) means the model is very good at identifying Class 0, though it has some "false alarms" (lower precision of 0.75). Also, the model has high Precision (0.94) means that when the model predicts Class 1, it is almost always correct, but it misses about 25% of the actual Class 1 cases (lower recall os 0.75).

5.2 Model 2: Decision Tree

In [39]:
dt = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
dt.fit(x_train, y_train)
y_pred_dt = dt.predict(x_test)
dt_acc = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_dt)
print("Decision Tree Accuracy:", dt_acc)

Decision Tree Accuracy: 0.7777777777777778
In [40]:
# Classification report
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_dt))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.67      1.00      0.80        16
           1       1.00      0.60      0.75        20

    accuracy                           0.78        36
   macro avg       0.83      0.80      0.78        36
weighted avg       0.85      0.78      0.77        36
In [41]:
# Confusion matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_dt)
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Greens')
plt.title("Decision Tree")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.show()

Insights

Decision Tree confusion matrix shows an accuracy of 77.8%. The model shows a slight imbalance, but not enough to severely skew results.

The model is "cautious" about predicting Class 1. It has perfect Precision (1.00), meaning everytime it predicted Class 1, it was correct. The Recall is lower (0.60), suggesting it missed about 40% of the actual Class 1 instances (False Negatives).

The model is noticeably biased towards the Class 0. It has high Recall (1.00), meaning it successfully identified every actual instance of Class 0. However, its Precision is lower (0.67), indicating that when it predicts Class 0, it is only correct abour 67% of the time (likely due to some False Positives).

Both the macro avg (0.78) and weighted avg (0.77) for the F1-score are very close, indicating the model's performance is relatively stable across both classes.

5.3 Model 3: Random Forest

In [42]:
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rf.fit(x_train, y_train)
y_pred_rf = rf.predict(x_test)
rf_acc = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_rf)
print("Random Forest Accuracy:", rf_acc)

Random Forest Accuracy: 0.8055555555555556
In [43]:
# Classification report
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_rf))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.71      0.94      0.81        16
           1       0.93      0.70      0.80        20

    accuracy                           0.81        36
   macro avg       0.82      0.82      0.81        36
weighted avg       0.84      0.81      0.80        36
In [44]:
# Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_rf)
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Oranges')
plt.title("Random Forest")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.show()

Insights

Random Forest confusion matrix shows an accuracy of ~80.6%. The dataset appears relatively balanced.

The Class O performance has high Recall (0.94), meaning the model is very good at identifying most of the actual positive instances of Class 0, though it has some "false alarms" (0.71 Precision).

The model is "conservative" when predicting Class 1. This class has high Precision (0.93), meaning when the model predicts Class 1, it is alomst always right. However, it misses about 30% of actual Class 1 cases (0.70 Recall).

The dataset is relatively balanced, making macro avg (0.81) a reliable metric for overall performance.

6. Model Comparison Report

6.1 Performance Table

In [45]:
model_accuracy = pd.DataFrame({
    'Model':['Logistic Regression',
             'Decision Tree',
             'Random Forest'],
    'Accuracy': [lr_acc,
                 dt_acc,
                 rf_acc]})
model_accuracy

# Sample prediction
sample=x.iloc[[0]]
prediction=lr.predict(sample)
print("Predicted Class:", prediction[0])

Predicted Class: 1

Insights

The sample is selected using integer-based indexing, and uses a logistic regression model(lr) to classify the input sample. The output indicates that the model has categorized this specific sample into Class 1.

In binary classification for heart disease, this typically signifies a positive finding (e.g., presence of heart disease).

TASK 2: PREDICTING MODEL STRATEGY

Selected Model: Logistic Regression is suggested for Heart Disease Prediction.

Justification: Logistic Regression serves as our parametric baseline model. It models the probability of heart disease using a linear combination of clinical features. The model shows an accuracy of ~80%. It achieves the highest accuracy among the tested algorithms. In clinical diagnostic, Recall (Sensitivity) is the primary metric to optimize. Missing a true heart disease patient (False Negative) presents a much higher risk than a False Positive. For example, missing a sick patient (False Negative) can lead to a fatal medical emergency. A False Positive simply triggers a harmless follow-up screening. Logistic Regression achieve the highest Recall (87.50%). This makes them the safest configurations for deployment.

For this specific dataset, a linear approach appears more effective or just as robust as complex ensemble methods like Random Forest. Random Forest serves as a strong backup or "challenger" model, as its performance is nearly identical to Logistic Regression.

TASK 3: STRATEGIC RECOMMENDATIONS FOR HOSPITAL

In corporate and clinical practice, predictive models must translate into actionable workflows. To maximize the patient safety and use these predictive assests effectively, hospitals should adopt the following procedures:

1. Real-time Diagnostic Triggers: Integrate the trained piple directly into the Electronic Health Records (EHR) framework to automatically lavel patients as high risk during routine checkups. When triage nurses input vitals and EKG readings, the model flags high-risk patients in real-time, escalating them for priority cardiac consultations.

2. Targeted Early Screening: Prioritize advanced cardiac imaging for individuals showing abnormalities in oldpeak_eq_st_depression or multiple blocked pathways in num_major_vessels . Use feature importance data to build early screening check-lists. Apply these simplified checklists during standard checkups for asymptomatic individuals over 50.

3. Proactive Lifestyle Adjustments: Automate wellness enrollments focusing on dietary sodium reduction and monitored exercises for male patients over 50 showing elevated resting_blood_pressure .

4. Digital Health Alerts: Implement a patient portal notification system. Group patients into Low, Moderate, and High-risk tiers based on the prediction probability output. If a patient's fasting clood sugar exceeds 120 mg/dl and they have high blood pressure, push atuomated preventative wellness advice. Patients flagged with high cardiovascular risk metrics - but who have not yet developed clinical disease - should be automatically enrolled in preventive care pathways (e.g., automated dietary tracking reminders, blood pressure checks, etc.).

5. Staff Allocation Tuning: Use model prediction trends to forecast higher cardiac admissions. Scale emergency room cardiology nursing staff accordingly during high-risk seasons.

6. Automated EHR Screening Trigger: Embed the finalized model directly into the Hospital's Electronic Health Record (EHR) ecosystem. When a practitioner enters basic blood work or vitals that surpass risk thresholds, the system flags the patient for an immediate cardiology consultation.

REPORT ON CHALLENGES FACED AND MITIGATION TECHNIQUES

1. Data Leakage Risk Mitigation

Challenge: Standardizing features using a global dataset mean and variance before splitting introduces lookahead bias, inflating performance metrics.

Mitigation: Implemented an encapsulation architecture to split dataset cleanly, computing descriptive properties only on training batches.

Reason: It is essential to provent any form of data leakage where information from the future (the test set) "leaks" into the training process. By standardizing features using only the training set's mean and variance - rather than the global dataset statistics - one ensures the model is evaluated on truly unseen data. This technique maintains the integrity of the performance metrics, providing a realtistic estimate of how the model will generalize to real-world, out-of-sample data.

2. Feature Scale Variance

Challenge: Features like serum_cholesterol_mg_per_dl and oldpeak_eq_st_depression operate on wildly different numeric ranges. Distance-based or regularized models could over-index on higher-valued matrics.

Mitigation: Incorporated Standard Scaler inside the numeric pipeline to normalize data mean to 0 and variance to 1.

Reason: Primarily, it is to ensure that machine learning models treat all features with equal importance, regardless of their original units or magnitude. By normalizing the data to a mean of 0 and a variance of 1, one prevents a specific feature from dominating the objective function and leading to faster convergence and more stable, accurate model performance.

3. Categorical String Processing (thal)

Challenge: The feature "thal" contains text labels (normal, fixed_defect, reversible_defect). Passing text directly into sklearn algorithms throws a value error.

Mitigation: Implemented Label Encoder to dynamically convert labels into numeric dummy variables during pipeline process.

Reason: It was implemented to bridge the gap between categorical string data and the numeric input. This treats each category as an independent binary vector without introducing artificial numerical hierarchies.

4. Imbalanced Categorical Representation

Challenge: The "thal" feature contained uneven distributions of structural defects across demographics.

Mitigation: Used stratified Train-Test Splits to guarantee that representation proportions of thallium defects remained perfectly uniform between training and validation blocks.

Reason: It ensures that the percentage of each category is the same in both the training set and the validation/test set as it is in in the original dataset. By guaranteeing that the validation block is representative of the training block, the performance metrics (like accuracy or F1-score) become more reliable and less prone to "luck of the draw" during data splitting.

5. Sensitivity to Outliers

Challenge: Extreme values in serum_cholesterol_mg_per_dl (above 400 mg/dl) distorted early trial models.

Mitigation: Applied a Robust Clipping Technique at the 1st and 99th percentiles rather than deleting data points, preserving valuable high-risk-clinical anomalies without skewing variance calculations.

Reason: It is used to handle extreme outliers in a dataset without losing valuable information. By restricting feature values to a consistent range, clipping helps stabilize the training process, reducing overfitting caused by a few rare, extreme values. In clinical trials, extreme values may be medically valid rather than errors. Clipping allows these cases to remain in the analysis while minimizing their potential to "distort early trial models".
